does: This paper addresses continual speaker identity unlearning in zero-shot text-to-speech systems, where privacy requests arrive sequentially rather than all at once. The authors demonstrate that existing unlearning methods fail in continual settings, reactivating previously unlearned speakers when new speakers are unlearned. They introduce CORTIS, a framework combining Fisher-information-based parameter masking with orthogonal projection to localize updates to speaker-relevant model weights while preventing interference with prior unlearning operations. The method achieves selective speaker removal without requiring access to previously-unlearned speaker data, maintaining privacy guarantees across sequential unlearning requests while preserving overall model performance on legitimate speakers.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in machine unlearning, which emerged from privacy-focused research into removing designated knowledge from trained models. It extends prior zero-shot text-to-speech unlearning methods that assumed batch unlearning scenarios by addressing the realistic continual learning setting. The paper combines classical techniques from the machine learning literature—Fisher information matrices for identifying important parameters and orthogonal projection for avoiding catastrophic forgetting—with speaker identity removal in generative models. This connects to broader work on continual learning and catastrophic interference, particularly how to preserve previously-learned constraints while acquiring new knowledge, adapted here to the privacy-critical domain of voice synthesis.
enables: This framework opens pathways for deployment of privacy-respecting speech synthesis systems in real-world scenarios where users request voice removal at different times. It establishes foundations for continual machine unlearning in other modalities and domains beyond speech, suggesting that orthogonal projection methods could address similar privacy-preserving sequential forgetting tasks in generative models. The work points toward systems that can maintain multiple privacy guarantees simultaneously without model retraining, enabling practical privacy-by-design in commercial text-to-speech services. It also motivates future investigation into more efficient orthogonal projection schemes and theoretical analysis of unlearning interference in high-dimensional neural network spaces.
